coredata.dc:title: Molecular interaction of fluorescent carbon dots from mature vinegar with human hemoglobin: Insights from spectroscopy, thermodynamics and AFM
coredata.dc:creator: Cao, Lin
Li, Jiaqi
Song, Yukun
Cong, Shuang
Wang, Haitao
Tan, Mingqian
coredata.dc:description: Foodborne nanoparticles have attracted considerable interest due to their distinctive fluorescence and physicochemical properties. The discovery of vinegar carbon dots (VCDs) has drawn our attention to study their effect on human plasma protein. Herein, spectral, constructional, morphological, and enzymatic activity assessments were carried out to investigate the interaction of VCDs with human hemoglobin (HHb). The intrinsic fluorescence of HHb was quenched significantly by the VCDs through a static quenching process. Furthermore, binding constants and important thermodynamic parameters were calculated, the negative enthalpy and entropy changes were accompanied by a negative Gibbs energy, which proposed the binding between VCDs with HHb was spontaneous. Moreover, negative enthalpy and entropy change corroborated the involvement of van der Waals force and hydrogen bonds in the binding process. Results from FTIR, atomic force microscopy and circular dichroism revealed change of HHB after binding with VCDs although their essential morphological features were unaffected. The esterase activity of HHb decreased after VCDs treatment in a dose-dependent manner, which further confirmed the effect of VCDs on HHb. The results offered detailed information about the interaction between VCDs and HHb.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Fluorescent carbon dots
Human hemoglobin
Interaction
Esterase activity

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Extraction of CDs from mature vinegar
Number of binding sites and association constant determination
Binding process between HHb and VCDs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) UV–vis absorption spectra of HHb (2 μmol L−1) in the presence of VCDs at ambient temperature in PBS buffer. The concentrations of VCDs (a-g) were 0, 6.5, 13.0, 19.5, 26.0, 32.5, 39.0 μmol L−1, respectively. Inset shows the porphyrin-Soret band of HHb. (B) The schematic diagram of the ultraviolet energy level transition of HHb.

Fluorescence spectra of HHb (2 μmol L−1) in the presence of different concentration of VCDs (a-g: 0, 6.5, 13.0, 19.5, 26.0, 32.5, 39.0 μmol L−1) at 298 K (A), 310 K (B) and 315 K (C). Stern-Volmer plot for HHb/VCDs at 298 K, 310 K and 315 K (D). Schematic diagram of fluorescence quenching of HHb at different temperatures (E).
(A) The plot of log[(F0 F)/F] inverse log[Q] of HHb upon addition of VCDs at 298, 310 and 315 K. (B) The logarithm of the equilibrium constant versus inverse temperature.

(A) FT-IR spectra of HHb and HHb-VCDs complex. CHHb = 2 μmol L−1, CCDs = 39 μmol L−1. (B) CD spectra of HHb and HHb-VCDs. The concentration of HHb was 2 μmol L−1. The concentrations of VCDs were 0–39 μmol L−1 (a–g), respectively.

The topography images and the corresponding three-dimensional AFM image of HHb and HHb-VCDs complex at different concentrations of VCDs. The topography images of HHb (A), HHb-VCDs-1 (C), HHb-VCDs-2 (E), HHb-VCDs-3 (G), HHb-VCDs-4 (I), HHb-VCDs-5 (K), and HHb-VCDs-6 (M). The three-dimensional AFM image of HHb (B), HHb-VCDs-1 (D), HHb-VCDs-2 (F), HHb-VCDs-3 (H), HHb-VCDs-4 (J), HHb-VCDs-5 (L), and HHb-VCDs-6 (N). The VCDs concentration for HHb-VCDs-1, HHb-VCDs-2, HHb-VCDs-3, HHb-VCDs-4, HHb-VCDs-5, and HHb-VCDs-6 was 6.5, 13, 19.5, 26, 32.5 and 39 μmol L−1, respectively. Diagram for morphological changes in HHb after the interaction with VCDs (O).
The parameters obtained from the Stern-Volmer plot for HHb/VCDs at 298 K, 310 K and 315 K.
Determination of Kb, n and thermodynamic parameters for the interaction of HHb with VCDs.

head.ce:title: Molecular interaction of fluorescent carbon dots from mature vinegar with human hemoglobin: Insights from spectroscopy, thermodynamics and AFM

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Food Science and Technology, National Engineering Research Center of Seafood, Dalian Polytechnic University, Qinggongyuan 1, Ganjingzi District, Dalian 116034, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Food Science and Technology
National Engineering Research Center of Seafood

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Food Science and Technology, National Engineering Research Center of Seafood, Dalian Polytechnic University, Qinggongyuan 1, Ganjingzi District, Dalian 116034, China

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Collaborative Innovation Center of Seafood Deep Processing, Dalian Polytechnic University, Dalian 116034, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Collaborative Innovation Center of Seafood Deep Processing

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Collaborative Innovation Center of Seafood Deep Processing, Dalian Polytechnic University, Dalian 116034, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: School of Food Science and Technology, National Engineering Research Center of Seafood, Dalian Polytechnic University, Qinggongyuan 1, Ganjingzi District, Dalian 116034, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Food Science and Technology
National Engineering Research Center of Seafood

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Foodborne nanoparticles have attracted considerable interest due to their distinctive fluorescence and physicochemical properties. The discovery of vinegar carbon dots (VCDs) has drawn our attention to study their effect on human plasma protein. Herein, spectral, constructional, morphological, and enzymatic activity assessments were carried out to investigate the interaction of VCDs with human hemoglobin (HHb). The intrinsic fluorescence of HHb was quenched significantly by the VCDs through a static quenching process. Furthermore, binding constants and important thermodynamic parameters were calculated, the negative enthalpy and entropy changes were accompanied by a negative Gibbs energy, which proposed the binding between VCDs with HHb was spontaneous. Moreover, negative enthalpy and entropy change corroborated the involvement of van der Waals force and hydrogen bonds in the binding process. Results from FTIR, atomic force microscopy and circular dichroism revealed change of HHB after binding with VCDs although their essential morphological features were unaffected. The esterase activity of HHb decreased after VCDs treatment in a dose-dependent manner, which further confirmed the effect of VCDs on HHb. The results offered detailed information about the interaction between VCDs and HHb.

Vinegar carbon dots (VCDs) interacted with human hemoglobin and decreased esterase-like activity.
Unlabelled Image

ce:section.ce:para: The safety issue of nanomaterials in foods is a long-lasting and hot topic [1,2]. Fluorescent carbon dots (CDs), a new kind of nanoparticles, have been discovered in various foods, including carbohydrate-based food caramels [3], commercial beverages [4,5], coffee [6], grilled hamburgers [7], milk and baked lambs [8,9]. The size of CDs in food items was usually less than 10 nm and bright fluorescence was observed under the irradiation of ultraviolet light [10]. Primary results suggested that the foodborne CDs may cause uncertainty or cytotoxicity on living cells [11,12]. For instance, the CDs formed during the Maillard reaction could enter HepG2 cells and lead to cell death via interfering glycolytic pathway [13]. However, the understanding about the health risk of these foodborne CDs was still limited.

Nanomaterials affect the protein structures or functions through physical binding under the physiological environment, which plays a critical role in nanotoxicity [14]. For example, metal-organic framework nanosheets (MOF) inhibited α-chymotrypsin activity effectively, and the kinetic studies suggested the interaction between MOF and α-chymotrypsin governed the inhibition process [15]. The CDs could also alter the function of protein since the lipase activity was inhibited after CDs addition [16]. Importantly, the interaction between proteins and CDs in food items was reported and the major forces involved in the interaction were proved to be hydrogen bonding and van der Waals forces [11]. Moreover, the CDs isolated from Coca-cola and Pepsi-cola were easily distributed in the digestive tract and were able to cross the blood-brain barrier and enter mouse brain [9]. This result suggests that CDs in food items can be transferred through blood circulation. Therefore, it is important to study the interaction between the protein in the blood and foodborne CDs.

Human hemoglobin (HHb) is the main component of red blood cells, which is composed of globin and heme [17,18]. Besides carrying oxygen and carbon dioxide, HHb could bind with many kinds of substances including exogenous and endogenous ones reversibly. It has been proved that certain exogenous drugs can interact with HHb [19]. Moreover, the interaction between HHb and nanomaterial has been demonstrated as well [20–22]. However, these studies were mainly focused on engineered nanoparticles, the interaction between foodborne nanostructures and HHb, was investigated rarely. Therefore, it is imperative to understand the binding of foodborne CDs with HHb and elucidate their interaction mechanism. The presence of Chinese vinegar carbon dots (VCDs) and their interaction with dopamine were demonstrated in our previous work [23]. However, as one of the most beloved traditional condiments, knowledge is still limited about the interaction between VCDs and HHb. Hence, there is an urgent need to investigate the biological effect of VCDs in blood.

The present work investigated the interaction between VCDs and HHb under physiological conditions. The binding process of HHb and VCDs was analyzed by various techniques to reveal the quenching mechanism of HHb, the number of binding sites and important thermodynamic parameters. The effects of VCDs on structure and function of HHb were examined, and the esterase activity of HHb was evaluated by the p-nitro-phenyl acetate method. The results shed light on the binding mechanism between HHb and VCDs, which provided fundamental information for evaluating the potential effect of nanoparticles from food items.

Sodium chloride, disodium hydrogen phosphate, potassium chloride, and potassium dihydrogen phosphate were purchased from Damao Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Tianjin, China). p-nitro-phenyl acetate was purchased from Aladdin Reagent Inc. (Shanghai, China). HHb was purchased from Sangon Biotech Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China) and dissolved in PBS (0.01 mol L−1, pH = 7.4). Chinese mature vinegar was purchased from the Walmart market (Zilin Food Co. Ltd., Shanxi, China), Dalian, China.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Extraction of CDs from mature vinegar
ce:section.ce:para: The CDs in Chinese mature vinegar were isolated according to a previously reported method [23]. The molecular weight of VCDs (m/z:964.73, Fig. S1) was measured by matrix-assisted laser desorption ionization time-of-flight mass spectrometry (MALDI-TOF, Autoflex II, Bruker Daltonics, Bremen, Germany). The morphology of VCDs was characterized by a transmission electron microscope (TEM). The images and the size distribution were shown in Fig. S2. The VCDs were well dispersed in water and the average size was 1.39 ± 0.29 nm.

Three-dimensional (3D) fluorescence, steady-state fluorescence, and synchronous fluorescence analysis were performed on F-2700 spectrometer (Hitachi, Tokyo, Japan) equipped with a thermostatic bath (SY-1000, Nanjing Xin Chen Biotechnology Co., Ltd., China) as a temperature controller. UV–vis absorption spectra range from 200 to 600 nm were recorded on a UV–vis spectrometer (Lambda 35, Perkin Elmer, Cambridge, MA, U.S.A.). FT-IR spectra were obtained by using a fourier transform infrared spectroscopy spectrometer (Perkin Elmer, Norwalk, CT, U.S.A.).

The fluorescence intensity of HHb was determined in the presence of VCDs at various temperature (298, 310 and 315 K), and the inner filter effect (IFE) was corrected according to Eq. (1):
(1)
F cor
=
F obs
×
e
A ex
+
A em
/ 2
where Fcor is fluorescence intensity after removing IFE and Fobs is the fluorescence intensity recorded by the fluorescence spectrophotometer. Aex and Aem are the optical densities at 280 nm (excitation wavelength) and 342 nm (emission wavelength).

The Stern-Volmer equation was introduced to analyze the fluorescence quenching process after removing the IFE:
(2)
F 0
F
= 1 +
k q
τ 0
Q
= 1 +
K sv
Q
where F0 is the fluorescence intensity in the absence of VCDs while F represents fluorescence intensity when VCDs is present (corrected by Eq. (1)). τ0 is the lifetime of HHb, kq is the quenching rate constant, [Q] is the concentration of VCDs, Ksv is Stern-Volmer quenching constant [24].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Number of binding sites and association constant determination
ce:section.ce:para:
The number of binding sites per protein (n) along with the apparent binding constant (Kb) was determined according to the modified Stern-Volmer Eq. (3) [25]:
(3)
log
F 0
− F
F
= log
K b
+ n log
Q

The enthalpy and entropy changes were calculated from the van't Hoff equation to clarify the nature of the binding process between HHb and VCDs.
(4)
ln
K b
= −
∆ H
RT
+
∆ S
R
where ∆H is enthalpy change while ∆S is entropy changes, R is the gas constant.

Gibbs free energy (ΔG) was determined according to Eq. (5).
(5)
∆G = ∆H − T∆S

The CD spectra of HHb and HHb-VCDs were determined on a JASCO J-1500 CD spectrometer at room temperature. The concentration of HHb (CHHb) was 2 μmol L−1. The concentration of VCDs (CCDs) was 6.5, 13.0, 19.5, 26.0, 32.5 and 39.0 μmol L−1, respectively.

Morphology studies of HHb and HHb-VCDs complexes were performed on AFM (AFM-5000, Hitachi, Tokyo, Japan). HHb solution (2 μmol L−1) and HHb-VCDs solution (CVCDs were from 6.5 to 39 μmol L−1) were filtered through a 0.22 μm filter membrane, dripped onto a mica sheet and dried at room temperature. Samples were imaged in dynamic force mode (DFM) with a silicon tip. The AFM images were processed using SelectorApp software.

Esterase activity of HHb was determined according to the previously reported procedure with minor modification [26]. Briefly, the reaction was initiated by adding 1.5 mmol L−1 p-nitro-phenyl acetate to a reaction mixture solution containing 2 μmol L−1 of HHb PBS solution in the presence of 0, 6.5, 39.0 μmol L−1 of VCDs, respectively, and absorbance change at 400 nm was recorded for esterase activity calculation.

UV–vis absorption spectroscopy is a powerful technique in characterizing protein structure change. Herein, the UV–vis absorption spectra of HHb in PBS buffer of pH 7.4 were recorded (Fig. 1A). The absorption band at 274 nm can be attributed to the tryptophan (Trp) and tyrosine (Tyr) residues. The characteristic absorption at 406 nm was associated with the n-π* transition within the heme group. The ultraviolet energy level transition of HHb is shown in Fig. 1B. The absorbance at 274 nm increased gradually with the increasing VCDs concentration. Meanwhile, the position of this peak remained unaltered, neither blue nor red shift was observed. The porphyrin-Soret band exhibited a small but significant change (about 4%) in the presence of VCDs (Fig. 1A inset). Such spectral changes can be interpreted by the alteration of the chemical environment surrounding the heme group [27]. The change in UV–vis absorption spectra of HHb indicated that the VCDs affect the heme environment and their overall structures, which could attribute to the interactions between VCDs and HHb [27].

Characteristic fluorescence spectroscopy is a powerful approach to obtain protein-ligand interaction. 3D fluorescence spectra were applied to provide useful information about the conformational change in HHb after binding with VCDs. As shown in Fig. S3, Peak a (λem = λex) and Peak b (λem = 2λex) were the first- and second-order Rayleigh scattering peaks, respectively. Generally, the fluorescence of protein was original from Trp or Tyr residues, which were sensitive to the microenvironment that surrounds them. Therefore, the fluorescence spectra were collected at room temperature in the range of 300–450 nm to evaluate the potential interaction between VCDs and HHb. Strong fluorescence was observed at 342 nm when excited at 280 nm, which was the characteristic emission of Trp and Tyr residues (Peak 1 in Fig. S3B, S3D) [28]. Additionally, there was the other Peak 2 (λex = 230 nm, λem = 342 nm, Fig. S3B, S3D) due to n → π* transition of the polypeptide backbone. The emission of Peak a shifted in the presence of VCDs (Fig. S3C). This result indicated that the VCDs changed the fluorescence of HHb, which further confirmed the result from UV–vis absorption spectra.

Simultaneously, conformational changes of HHb consequent to the binding of VCDs were investigated using synchronous fluorescence spectroscopy (Fig. S4). The synchronous fluorescence of HHb was collected for the Trp and Tyr residues by fixing the scanning interval between the excitation and emissions wavelengths at 60 nm and 15 nm, respectively. The results showed that the synchronous fluorescence of HHb was quenched significantly by VCDs as the concentration of the VCDs increased from 0 to 39.0 μmol L−1, however, no obvious shift was observed in the maximum emission, suggesting the microenvironment surrounding the Tyr and Trp residues was unaffected during the binding process. [29]

Generally, there are two mechanisms (statics and dynamics) involved in fluorescence quenching, which can be distinguished by their fluorescence behavior. [25] In dynamic quenching mechanisms, temperature increase can enhance the quenching constants since the faster diffusion results in a more considerable amount of collision. In contrast, higher temperature weakens the interaction between quencher and fluorophore in the static quenching, and the fluorophore-quencher complex trend to dissociate, causing a decrease in quenching constants subsequently. Previous results suggested that the CDs could quench the intrinsic fluorescence of proteins through statics [30] or dynamics quenching [31]. To clarify the fluorescence quenching mechanism of HHb with VCDs, a steady-state fluorescence approach was applied. The effect of VCDs on fluorescence intensity of HHb at 298, 310 and 315 K was shown in Fig. 2A, B and C, respectively. The fluorescence of HHb was quenched obviously by VCDs when excited at 280 nm. No obvious blue-shifting was observed at all the testing temperatures. Furthermore, the fluorescence intensity was corrected according to Eq. (1) to remove the inner filter effect. The plots of Stern-Volmer equation were liner (Fig. 2D) at all the tested temperatures. The corresponding kq values in Table 1 shows that the kq values range from 1.47 × 1011 to 1.15 × 1011 mol L−1 s−1, which are much higher than the maximum diffusion collision quenching rate (2 × 105 mol L−1 s−1) of quencher with biopolymer [32]. Furthermore, the kq values decreased from 1.47 × 1011 to 1.15 × 1011 mol L−1 s−1 as the temperature increased from 298 to 315 K, while the Ksv values decreased from 1.47 × 104 to 1.15 × 104 mol L−1 at the same time. This result suggested that fluorescence quenching of HHb by VCDs may mainly cause by static quenching. The fluorescence lifetime of HHb was measured to further confirm the quenching mechanism (Fig. S5). The fluorescence lifetimes of HHb and HHb-VCDs complex were 3.69 ns and 3.67 ns, respectively. There was no obvious decrease of HHb lifetime after binding with VCDs, revealing the collision between HHb and VCDs could be neglected.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Binding process between HHb and VCDs
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 3A shows the typical double-logarithm plot of log[(F0-Fcor)/Fcor] versus log[Q] at various temperatures. The binding constant (Kb) and the number of binding sites (n) were obtained from intercept and slope, respectively (Table 2 ). The Kb values decrease from 3.06 × 105 to 3.40 × 103 as the temperature increase from 298 to 315 K, indicating the HHb-VCDs complex was destabilized at a higher temperature in the system. Additionally, the values of n were calculated to be approximately equal to 1, suggesting only one binding site was present on HHb for VCDs to form the ground-state complex. This result was comparable to the previous reports. For instants, the binding stoichiometry of CDs derived from roast duck with human serum albumin was 0.659 [29]. Similarly, about 2 CDs from thermal-processed fish binding with 1 trypsin molecule [33].

The thermodynamic parameters reflect the type of binding forces (hydrogen bonds, hydrophobic, van der Waals force, or electrostatic interactions) when the exogenous components are added to HHb. Both ΔH and ΔS were positive when a typical hydrophobic interaction occurred [34]. On the contrary, negative ΔH and ΔS value indicated van der Waals force and hydrogen bonds were the main acting force [33]. To confirm the interaction force between HHb and VCDs, the thermodynamic parameters were analyzed. A good linear relationship between lnKb and 1/T was determined from the van't Hoff plots (Fig. 3B). The ΔH, ΔS, and ΔG values at different temperatures were calculated and summarized in Table 2. The ΔG values were negative at different conditions, indicating the interactions between VCDs and HHb were spontaneous. Both ΔH and ΔS are negative, which indicated that the reaction is an enthalpy-driven and exothermic process. Furthermore, these results suggested hydrogen interaction and van der Waals force played a key role between VCDs and HHb interaction. Also, the mean zeta potential of HHb in PBS buffer was about 2 mV, while the mean zeta potential of VCDs and the HHb-VCDs complex was −1.13 mV and 0.33 mV respectively (Fig. S6). The change of zeta potential indicated electrostatic interactions exist in the reaction as well, although to a low extent. The charged residues on the surface of HHb probably contributed to the electrostatic interaction between VCDs and HHb. The electrostatic interaction may influence HHb binding and thus facilitating Van der Waals or hydrogen bond interaction in the presence of VCDs. To sum up, van der Waals forces, hydrogen bond, and electrostatic interactions were involved in the spontaneous binding process between VCDs and HHb, among which, hydrogen bond and van der Waals forces played major roles. This was consistent with previous result of binding process between human serum albumin and CDs [35]. Moreover, the interaction between CDs from thermal-processed fish and pepsin was mainly derived by van der Waals and hydrogen bonds as well [32]. Some kinds of CDs trend to interact with proteins through hydrophobic interactions, [27] suggesting their driving force for interaction was dependent on the properties of CDs and proteins.

The interaction between HHb and VCDs may alternate the backbone and polar side-chains of HHb. Those conformational changes could be reflecting in spectral properties in the infrared region. Therefore, FT-IR spectroscopy was introduced to check whether those interactions change the structure of HHb. FT-IR spectra of HHb and HHb-VCDs complex are shown in Fig. 4A. Amide I band was the major vibrational band in HHb. Upon VCDs addition, the CO stretching vibration of HHb decreased in the presence of VCDs, demonstrating the CO group in the polypeptide backbone of HHb involved in the interaction (Fig. 4A). The peaks at 1538 cm−1 and 1362 cm−1 are amide II and amide III band, respectively, and the VCDs did not induce a significant peak shift in amide II and amide III, suggesting NH bending and CN stretching vibration was not affected by VCDs. The change in the amide I band in the presence of VCDs suggesting conformational change occurred in HHb [36,37]. Notably, the FT-IR spectra of HHb and HHb-VCDs were similar, which indicated the essential characteristics of the HHb secondary structure were retained even in the presence of VCDs at the testing concentration.

CD spectroscopy is the most extensive method to determine the secondary structure of the protein. The CD spectrum of HHb contained two negative minima in the far UV region at 208 and 222 nm (Fig. 4B), which demonstrated the presence of α-helical structure in HHb. [27] The broad negative minima around 208 nm suggested α-helix were the dominant structures. The CD spectra of HHb changed slightly after adding VCDs, suggesting the changes in the secondary structure of HHb may occur. Furthermore, the content of secondary structures in HHb, including α-helix, β-sheet, β-turn and random coil, was calculated through fitting the CD spectra by SpectraManager program, and their contents were summarized in Table S1. The amount of α-helix decreased from 64.2% to around 62.8% upon binding with VCDs, meanwhile, the content of the β-sheet, β-turn and random coil in HHb increased slightly. However, the tiny change of secondary structures in HHb was statistical insignificance, indicating the VCDs caused an indistinctive change of α-helix, β-sheet, β-turn and random coil for the HHb. These results were in line with the results obtained from FT-IR, which further demonstrated binding with VCDs induced slight change in HHb.

DSC was performed to investigate the effects of VCDs on the denaturation temperature of HHb. The DSC curves of HHb and HHb-VCDs (Fig. S7) exhibit that the denaturation temperature of HHb changed from 72.49 °C to 68.37 °C after adding VCDs. The decrease of denaturation temperature suggested the non-covalent interaction of HHb by VCDs and thus reducing the thermal stability decrease of HHb. [38]

The aggregation and morphological change in HHb after the interaction with VCDs were monitored by AFM. Fig. 5A represents topography images of HHb, while Fig. 5B showing the corresponding three-dimensional AFM image, which revealed the morphology of HHb was sharp and the height of HHb was about 10 nm. The three-dimensional length of HHb was about 6.0, 8.8, and 10.4 nm, respectively, according to crystallographic analysis [39]. The results from AFM were consistent with that from the crystal structure, suggesting HHb was well dispersed. Fig. 5C–N was the AFM images of the HHb-VCDs complex and their corresponding particle sizes analysis was shown in Fig. S8. The height of the HHb-VCDs complex increased slightly when the concentration of VCDs was 6.5 μmol L−1, which might be attributed to the attachment of VCDs on the surface of HHb since the peak shape of the HHb-VCDs complex became smooth. When the concentration of VCDs reached 13.0 μmol L−1 (HHb-VCDs-2), the height of the HHb-VCDs complex was about 20 nm, which was 2-times higher as compared to that of the HHb. Further incensement in VCDs concentration did not lead to an increase in particle size. The results from AFM validated that binding with VCDs led to aggregation of HHb (Fig. 5O), which may affect their biological functions.

The influence of nanoparticles, especially CDs, on enzyme activity has been reported previously. For example, chiral CDs could inhibit the activity of maltose effectively, which could be attributed to their interaction [40]. Furthermore, the effects of foodborne CDs on enzyme activity have drawn considerable attention. It was reported that the CDs separated from roast fish inhibited both pepsin and trypsin activities significantly. Since those CDs could bind with pepsin and trypsin, the enzyme activity decrease may be one of the consequences of CDs induced conformational change. [32]

In this study, the esterase-like activity of HHb was investigated to reveal the influence of VCDs on HHb function. Changes in the relative esterase-like activity of HHb with increasing concentration of VCDs were shown in Fig. S9. The esterase-like activity of free HHb was defined as 100%, while the activity of HHb-VCDs decreased to 87.8% when the concentration of VCDs reached 39 μmol L−1. This result affirmed that the VCDs might induce the enzyme activity of HHb thus changing its function.

In summary, the molecular interaction between HHb and VCDs was investigated by spectroscopic, thermodynamic and microscopic analysis. First, the fluorescence spectroscopy results indicated that the static quenching mechanism played a major role in fluorescence quenching of HHb with VCDs. Hydrogen bond and van der Waal interactions were the main forces between HHb and VCDs. The negative value of the Gibbs energy indicated the spontaneous nature of the binding interaction. Second, FT-IR and CD spectroscopy suggested that the VCDs might induce slight change of HHb. The AFM analysis suggested that the HHb aggregated after binding with VCDs. The enzymatic activity of HHb decreased upon the interaction with VCDs. In summary, this study provided new insights into the interaction of CDs derived from mature vinegar with HHb, which may be useful in understanding the potential adverse effects of foodborne CDs.

Cao Lin: Investigation, Writing - Original Draft. Li Jiaqi: Data curation, Formal analysis. Song Yukun: Formal analysis. Cong Shuang : Methodology. Wang Haitao: Writing - Review & Editing, Conceptualization. Tan Mingqian: Writing- Reviewing and Editing, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare no competing financial interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Central Funds Guiding the Local Science and Technology Development of China (2020JH6/10500002), the National Nature Science Foundation of China (31872915) and the National Key Research and Development Project (2017YFC1600702).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: MALDI-TOF-MS spectra, TEM image and size distribution of vinegar carbon dots (VCDs); 3D fluorescence spectra, synchronous fluorescence spectra, fluorescence lifetime decay curves, Zeta potential, DSC thermograms, particle size analysis of human hemoglobin (HHb) and HHb-VCDs; Relative activity of esterase, quantitative analysis of the change of secondary structural content of HHb in presence of VCDs.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijbiomac.2020.11.203.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Is nano safe in foods? Establishing the factors impacting the gastrointestinal fate and toxicity of organic and inorganic food-grade nanoparticles
